**Luigi Longo** (15 March 1900 – 16 October 1980), also known as **Gallo**, was an Italian [communist](/wiki/Communist "Communist") politician and [general secretary](/wiki/General_secretary "General secretary") of the [Italian Communist Party](/wiki/Italian_Communist_Party "Italian Communist Party") from 1964 to 1972.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Luigi_Longo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Longo was born in [Fubine](/wiki/Fubine "Fubine"), in the [province of Alessandria](/wiki/Province_of_Alessandria "Province of Alessandria"), [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont "Piedmont"). As a student at the [Politecnico di Torino](/wiki/Politecnico_di_Torino "Politecnico di Torino"), he became active in the youth wing of the [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party") (PSI), and engaged in political [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda "Propaganda") from a [Marxist](/wiki/Marxist "Marxist") perspective. He was a regular visitor to the offices of *[L'Ordine Nuovo](/wiki/L%27Ordine_Nuovo "L'Ordine Nuovo")*, the newspaper founded by [Antonio Gramsci](/wiki/Antonio_Gramsci "Antonio Gramsci"), and became acquainted with Gramsci and [Palmiro Togliatti](/wiki/Palmiro_Togliatti "Palmiro Togliatti"). In 1921, at the [Livorno Congress](/wiki/Livorno_Congress "Livorno Congress") of the PSI, he was one of the instigators of the split in the party, when supporters of [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin")'s [Bolshevik](/wiki/Bolshevik "Bolshevik") line left to form the Italian Communist Party (PCI). He became a leading figure in the new PCI along with Togliatti, Gramsci and others.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Foto_segnaletica_di_Luigi_Longo.jpg/250px-Foto_segnaletica_di_Luigi_Longo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Foto_segnaletica_di_Luigi_Longo.jpg)

Photo of Longo after his arrest in 1930

Longo was a fervent [anti-fascist](/wiki/Anti-fascism "Anti-fascism"), and, when [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") established his [Fascist regime](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism") in Italy in 1922, he emigrated to [France](/wiki/France "France") where he became one of the principal leaders of the PCI. In the same year, he was a member of a delegation to the [Comintern](/wiki/Comintern "Comintern") Congress in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), where he met Lenin. He would return to Moscow several times in the years to come, with a specific expertise in political ideology, and was to meet [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") and other members of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") leadership. In 1933 he became a member of the Comintern's political commission. In 1934 he signed a joint action agreement between the PCI and the PSI.

## Spanish Civil War and Resistance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Luigi_Longo&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Spanish Civil War and Resistance")]

Longo took part in the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") as an inspector of the [Republican troops](/wiki/Spanish_Republican_Army "Spanish Republican Army") in the [International Brigades](/wiki/International_Brigades "International Brigades") under the leadership of [Randolfo Pacciardi](/wiki/Randolfo_Pacciardi "Randolfo Pacciardi"), and took the [nom de guerre](/wiki/Nom_de_guerre "Nom de guerre") *Gallo*. After the defeat of the [Second Spanish Republic](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic") by [General Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco"), he returned to France.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Luigi_Longo_and_Pietro_Nenni.jpg/250px-Luigi_Longo_and_Pietro_Nenni.jpg)](/wiki/File:Luigi_Longo_and_Pietro_Nenni.jpg)

Longo and [Pietro Nenni](/wiki/Pietro_Nenni "Pietro Nenni") during the Spanish Civil War

Following the [1940 invasion of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France"), the [Vichy-based](/wiki/Vichy_France "Vichy France") collaborationist government was established under [Philippe Pétain](/wiki/Philippe_P%C3%A9tain "Philippe Pétain"). Longo was arrested and detained in an [internment camp](/wiki/Internment_camp "Internment camp") at [Vernet](/wiki/Camp_Vernet "Camp Vernet") from 1939 to 1941. There he made the acquaintance of [Leo Valiani](/wiki/Leo_Valiani "Leo Valiani"), among other left-wing radicals. In 1941 he was handed over to the Italian fascist authorities and interned at [Ventotene](/wiki/Ventotene "Ventotene"). When Mussolini [fell from power](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Fascist_regime_in_Italy "Fall of the Fascist regime in Italy") on 25 July 1943, Longo was released. After Mussolini regained control of [Northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy") (which he led as the [Italian Social Republic](/wiki/Italian_Social_Republic "Italian Social Republic")), Longo took command of the [Garibaldi Brigades](/wiki/Garibaldi_Brigades "Garibaldi Brigades"), the communist forces in the [Italian partisan resistance](/wiki/Italian_resistance_movement "Italian resistance movement"). Longo became deputy commander of the *Gruppo volontari per la liberta* ("Group of Volunteers for Freedom"), and a close collaborator of [Ferruccio Parri](/wiki/Ferruccio_Parri "Ferruccio Parri"); in April 1945 Longo was one of the leading figures of the uprising in northern Italy. It was at Dongo on Lake Como on 28 April 1945 and whilst being escorted by the Garibaldi Brigade partisans that Mussolini and his mistress [Claretta Petacci](/wiki/Claretta_Petacci "Claretta Petacci") were executed; the extent to which Longo took part in the killings has been the subject of dispute by historians.

## Post-war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Luigi_Longo&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Post-war")]

After the war, he was a member of the National Congress and in 1946 was elected to the [Constituent Assembly](/wiki/Constituent_Assembly "Constituent Assembly"). Also in 1946, [Teresa Mattei](/wiki/Teresa_Mattei "Teresa Mattei") chose the mimosa as the symbol of [International Women's Day](/wiki/International_Women%27s_Day "International Women's Day")[[1]](#cite_note-bbb-1) at the request of Longo.[[2]](#cite_note-florentine-2) He was subsequently elected, and repeatedly re-elected, to the [Italian Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Italian_Chamber_of_Deputies "Italian Chamber of Deputies") on the PCI list and was a member of the party leadership. In 1964, after the death of Palmiro Togliatti, he became secretary of the PCI, declaring that he was "a secretary, not a boss". In this role, he continued Togliatti's line, known as the "Italian road to [Socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism")", playing down the alliance between the Italian Communist Party and the Soviet Union. He reacted without hostility to the new left movements that sprang up in 1968 and, among the leaders of the PCI, was one of those most disposed to engage with the new activists, although he did not condone their excesses.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/LongoTogliatti.jpg/250px-LongoTogliatti.jpg)](/wiki/File:LongoTogliatti.jpg)

Longo and Togliatti during the VIII Congress of the PCI, 1956

In late 1968 Longo suffered a [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke"); although he partially recovered in the subsequent months, from February 1969 he was assisted in most decisions by [Enrico Berlinguer](/wiki/Enrico_Berlinguer "Enrico Berlinguer") acting as vice-secretary. In 1972 Longo resigned from the position of party secretary, supporting the choice of Berlinguer as his successor. From that year until his death, eight years later in Rome, he was honorary president of the PCI. In that capacity, he expressed his opposition to the "national solidarity" line the PCI was later to espouse.

Longo was also a prolific writer. He was the founder of *[Vie Nuove](/wiki/Vie_Nuove "Vie Nuove")*, a popular magazine of the Communist Party.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
